mnemonics.title: RE vs Recursive Memory Aid
mnemonics.content: R.E. = Run Eternally (may loop forever). Recursive = Really Stops (always halts). Think: 'Recursive Really Ceases'.

mnemonics.title: Rice's Theorem Memory
mnemonics.content: RICE: 'Really Interesting properties are Computationally Impossible except trivial ones'. Non-trivial = not all TMs have it, not all TMs lack it.

mnemonics.title: Halting Problem Proof
mnemonics.content: DIAGONAL: Assume decider H exists → Build paradoxical machine D → D halts iff it doesn't → contradiction → H cannot exist.

acronyms.usage: Language recognized by a TM that may loop forever on non-members. Also called Turing-recognizable or semi-decidable.
The fundamental model of computation - a theoretical device that can simulate any algorithmic process.
A TM that can simulate any other TM given its description. Basis of stored-program computers.
Same as Turing-recognizable - language for which a TM exists that accepts all and only members.
Languages whose complements are RE. The complement of the Halting Problem is a classic example.

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a spectrum: Regular < CFL < Decidable (Recursive) < RE < All Languages. As we move right, more languages exist but fewer are decidable.

Picture a grid: rows = all TMs, columns = all inputs. Cell (i,j) = what TM i does on input j (accept/reject/loop). Build D that does opposite of diagonal entry. D's behavior at diagonal is paradoxical.
Draw a circle representing 'all properties of TM languages'. Divide into 'trivial' (always true/false) and 'non-trivial'. Mark non-trivial as 'UNDECIDABLE' territory.
Think of RE languages like a club: Union of two RE members = in club. Intersection = in club. Complement = OUT of club (unless decidable). Not closed under complement!

analogies.analogy: Decidable is like having a definite answer key - for any question, you get YES or NO. Undecidable is like having a test where some answers might never come (the student keeps thinking forever).
Think of RE like a search engine: it will find your query if it exists (accept), but if it doesn't exist, you might wait forever (infinite loop) or get 'no results' (reject).
Like saying: except for trivial statements like 'this book has words' or 'this book has no words', you cannot algorithmically verify interesting properties about what books mean.
Like trying to predict your own future: to know if you'll finish a task, you'd need to actually do it (or think about doing it, creating infinite regression).
